intent: Check whether the candidate can define REST as an architectural style and explain the major constraints beyond simply saying HTTP JSON API.
primaryQuestion: What is REST, and what are the constraints of REST?

expectedSignals.label: Defines REST as an architectural style for networked systems built around resources, representations, uniform interfaces, and standard protocol semantics.

requirements.description: States that REST is an architectural style rather than only JSON over HTTP.
Connects REST to resources, representations, uniform interface, and protocol semantics.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes REST from a concrete protocol or library; HTTP is the most common implementation context, but REST itself is a style.

requirements.description: Explains that REST is not a specific framework, route library, or protocol implementation.
Acknowledges HTTP as the usual REST API context without reducing REST to HTTP endpoints.

expectedSignals.label: Names the core REST constraints: client-server separation, statelessness, cacheability, uniform interface, layered system, and optional code-on-demand.

requirements.description: Names most core constraints: client-server, stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered system, and optional code-on-demand.
Avoids treating CRUD routes alone as the full definition of REST constraints.

expectedSignals.label: Explains uniform interface through resource identifiers, representations, self-descriptive messages, and hypermedia as the engine of application state at a conceptual level.

requirements.description: Explains uniform interface using resource identifiers, representations, and self-descriptive messages.
Mentions hypermedia/HATEOAS at a conceptual level without needing deep implementation detail.

expectedSignals.label: Connects the constraints to practical API properties such as scalability, evolvability, independent client/server development, and intermediary support.

requirements.description: Explains how statelessness, cacheability, or layering supports scaling, caches, proxies, or gateways.
Explains how uniform contracts and client-server separation support independent evolution.

scoringHints.description: Defines REST accurately, names the constraints, explains why they exist, and connects them to practical API design.
Knows REST is resource-oriented over HTTP and can explain several constraints, but misses HATEOAS or layered/cache details.
Describes common HTTP endpoint patterns but does not separate REST principles from implementation habits.
Answer is too vague or incorrect to show REST fundamentals.

followUps.question: Can you explain one REST constraint and show how it changes the way an API endpoint is designed?
followUps.purpose: Move the answer from memorized keywords to applied understanding.

followUps.question: Why does cacheability matter for a public API, and which HTTP features help with it?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate connects REST constraints to HTTP behavior.

en.title: REST definition and constraints
en.displayQuestion: What is REST, and what are the constraints of REST?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands REST as an architectural style, not just HTTP endpoints.
guidance: Start with resources, representations, and uniform interface.
Name client-server, stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered system, and optional code-on-demand.
Explain why these constraints improve scalability, evolvability, and intermediary support.
Avoid reducing REST to JSON or CRUD naming only.
commonMistakes: Calling every HTTP JSON API RESTful.
Forgetting statelessness or cacheability.
Explaining only route naming without architectural constraints.
Ignoring uniform interface semantics.

vi.title: Định nghĩa REST và các ràng buộc
vi.displayQuestion: REST là gì? Các ràng buộc của REST là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu REST là một architectural style, không chỉ là các endpoint HTTP.
guidance: Bắt đầu từ resource, representation và uniform interface.
Nêu client-server, stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered system và code-on-demand tùy chọn.
Giải thích vì sao các ràng buộc này giúp hệ thống dễ scale, dễ tiến hóa và tận dụng intermediary như cache/proxy.
Không thu hẹp REST thành JSON hoặc cách đặt tên CRUD.
commonMistakes: Gọi mọi HTTP JSON API là RESTful.
Quên statelessness hoặc cacheability.
Chỉ nói về cách đặt route mà bỏ qua constraint kiến trúc.
Bỏ qua ý nghĩa của uniform interface.

ja.title: REST の定義と制約
ja.displayQuestion: REST とは何ですか。REST の constraints には何がありますか。
ja.displayIntent: REST を単なる HTTP endpoint ではなく architectural style として理解しているか確認する。
guidance: resource、representation、uniform interface から説明する。
client-server、stateless、cacheable、uniform interface、layered system、任意の code-on-demand を挙げる。
これらの制約が scalability、evolvability、intermediary support にどう効くか説明する。
REST を JSON や CRUD の命名規則だけに縮小しない。
commonMistakes: すべての HTTP JSON API を RESTful と呼ぶ。
statelessness や cacheability を忘れる。
route naming だけを説明し、architecture constraints を無視する。
uniform interface の意味を説明しない。

sourceReferences.label: Roy Fielding dissertation: REST architectural style
sourceReferences.note: Used for the original REST constraints and architectural framing.

sourceReferences.label: MDN: HTTP overview
sourceReferences.note: Used for HTTP resource, message, and intermediary context.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands stateless REST requests, including the difference between application state on the client and resource state on the server.
primaryQuestion: What does stateless mean in REST?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that each request must contain enough information for the server to understand and process it without relying on server-side conversational session state from previous requests.

requirements.description: States that each request must carry the context needed to process it.
Explains that the server should not depend on prior conversational request state to understand the next request.

expectedSignals.label: Clarifies that the server can still store resource state such as users, orders, and tokens; statelessness is about request context, not having no database.

requirements.description: Separates persisted resource state from per-client conversational session state.
Clarifies that stateless REST can still use databases, users, orders, tokens, or other stored resources.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that authentication context is sent with each request, for example through a bearer token, cookie, or signed credential, and then verified server-side.

requirements.description: States that credentials or auth context are provided on each request.
Gives examples such as bearer tokens, cookies, signed credentials, or API keys and server-side verification.

expectedSignals.label: Connects statelessness to horizontal scaling, load balancing, retry behavior, and simpler failure recovery.

requirements.description: Explains why stateless handling works better with multiple instances or load balancers.
Mentions retry behavior, failover, or simpler recovery when no instance-local session is required.

expectedSignals.label: Acknowledges trade-offs such as larger requests, repeated authentication checks, and careful token/session invalidation design.

requirements.description: Mentions costs such as larger requests or repeated auth verification.
Acknowledges token/session invalidation, revocation, expiry, or credential rotation complexity.

scoringHints.description: Separates resource state from session context, explains per-request credentials, and connects statelessness to scaling and reliability.
Understands independent requests but misses one nuance such as server resource state or authentication trade-offs.
Repeats that REST is stateless without being able to explain what the server may still store.
Answer confuses statelessness with no persistence or no authentication.

followUps.question: If REST is stateless, can the server still store a user record in a database? Why?
followUps.purpose: Correct confusion between resource state and session state.

followUps.question: What changes operationally when a stateless API is deployed behind multiple load-balanced instances?

en.title: Stateless REST requests
en.displayQuestion: What does stateless mean in REST?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands independent requests and does not confuse statelessness with no persistence.
guidance: Explain that each request carries the context needed to process it.
Separate server-side resource state from conversational session state.
Mention authentication credentials being sent and validated per request.
Discuss scaling and load-balancing benefits.
commonMistakes: Thinking stateless means no database.
Depending on in-memory server session state without naming the trade-off.
Forgetting per-request authentication context.
Ignoring retries and load balancing.

vi.title: Request stateless trong REST
vi.displayQuestion: Stateless trong REST nghĩa là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên hiểu request độc lập và không nhầm stateless với việc không có persistence.
guidance: Giải thích mỗi request phải mang đủ context để server xử lý.
Tách resource state trên server khỏi conversational session state.
Nhắc việc credential xác thực được gửi và verify theo từng request.
Nói về lợi ích khi scale ngang và load balancing.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ stateless nghĩa là không được dùng database.
Phụ thuộc vào in-memory session state mà không nêu trade-off.
Quên authentication context theo từng request.
Bỏ qua retry và load balancing.

ja.title: REST の stateless request
ja.displayQuestion: REST における stateless とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: 独立した request を理解し、stateless を persistence 不可と混同していないか確認する。
guidance: 各 request が処理に必要な context を持つことを説明する。
server-side resource state と conversational session state を分ける。
authentication credentials を request ごとに送り検証することに触れる。
horizontal scaling と load balancing の利点を説明する。
commonMistakes: stateless は database を使えないという意味だと考える。
trade-off を説明せず in-memory session state に依存する。
request ごとの authentication context を忘れる。
retry や load balancing への影響を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: Roy Fielding dissertation: Stateless constraint
sourceReferences.note: Used for stateless request semantics.

sourceReferences.label: MDN: HTTP authentication
sourceReferences.note: Used for per-request authentication context.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can design a resource-oriented CRUD URL set for users and map HTTP methods to resource operations correctly.
primaryQuestion: How would you design URLs for a users resource with full CRUD support?

expectedSignals.label: Uses a collection resource such as GET /users for listing and POST /users for creation.

requirements.description: Uses /users as the collection resource.
Maps GET /users to list and POST /users to create.

expectedSignals.label: Uses an item resource such as GET /users/:id for retrieval, PUT or PATCH /users/:id for update, and DELETE /users/:id for deletion.

requirements.description: Uses /users/:id or an equivalent stable item URI for a single user.
Maps GET, PUT/PATCH, and DELETE on the item URI to retrieve, update, and delete.

expectedSignals.label: Keeps actions out of URLs for normal CRUD and lets HTTP methods express the operation.

requirements.description: Avoids paths such as /getUsers, /createUser, or /deleteUser for normal CRUD.
Explains that HTTP methods express the CRUD operation.

expectedSignals.label: Uses query parameters for filtering, searching, sorting, field selection, and pagination rather than inventing action paths.

requirements.description: Places search, filters, sorting, fields, page, or limit in query parameters.
Avoids creating action paths such as /users/searchByEmail for ordinary list modifiers.

expectedSignals.label: Handles nested resources carefully, for example GET /users/:id/orders when orders are scoped to a user, while avoiding overly deep URL hierarchies.

requirements.description: Uses nested URLs when the child resource is naturally scoped by the parent.
Mentions avoiding deeply nested paths when a query parameter or top-level resource is clearer.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions appropriate status codes such as 201 for creation, 200 for read/update with body, 204 for successful delete with no body, 400 for invalid input, and 404 for missing resources.

requirements.description: Uses appropriate success codes such as 201, 200, or 204 for create/read/update/delete cases.
Mentions client error codes such as 400 for invalid input and 404 for missing resources.

scoringHints.description: Maps collection and item URLs to methods, uses query parameters well, handles nested resources carefully, and mentions response status behavior.
Gets the core CRUD routes right but misses pagination/filtering or nested-resource nuance.
Knows common routes but mixes verbs into paths or cannot explain method responsibilities.
Route design does not show REST resource modeling.

followUps.question: Where would you put search, sort, page, and limit for the users list endpoint?
When would /users/:id/orders be better than /orders?userId=:id?

en.title: Users CRUD resource URLs
en.displayQuestion: How would you design URLs for a users resource with full CRUD support?
en.displayIntent: Check practical REST URL modeling for collection and item resources.
guidance: Use /users for the collection and /users/:id for one user.
Map GET, POST, PUT/PATCH, and DELETE to the right resource operations.
Use query parameters for list options such as search, filters, sorting, and pagination.
Mention expected status codes and response shapes.
commonMistakes: Putting verbs into paths like /createUser.
Using POST for everything.
Encoding filters as path segments.
Over-nesting resources.

vi.title: URL CRUD cho resource users
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để thiết kế URL cho resource users với CRUD đầy đủ?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng model URL REST thực tế cho collection và item resource.
guidance: Dùng /users cho collection và /users/:id cho một user.
Map GET, POST, PUT/PATCH và DELETE vào đúng operation trên resource.
Dùng query parameter cho search, filter, sort và pagination của danh sách.
Nhắc status code và response shape mong đợi.
commonMistakes: Đưa động từ vào path như /createUser.
Dùng POST cho mọi thao tác.
Đưa filter vào path segment.
Nested resource quá sâu.

ja.title: users resource の CRUD URL
ja.displayQuestion: users resource に対して full CRUD を提供する URL をどう設計しますか。
ja.displayIntent: collection と item resource の実践的な REST URL modeling を確認する。
guidance: collection には /users、単一 user には /users/:id を使う。
GET、POST、PUT/PATCH、DELETE を適切な resource operation に対応させる。
search、filter、sort、pagination などの list option は query parameter に置く。
期待する status code と response shape に触れる。
commonMistakes: /createUser のように path に動詞を入れる。
すべての操作に POST を使う。
filter を path segment に入れる。
resource を深く nested しすぎる。

sourceReferences.label: MDN: HTTP request methods
sourceReferences.note: Used for method semantics.

sourceReferences.label: Microsoft REST API Guidelines
sourceReferences.note: Used for resource URL and collection conventions.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate knows common REST route naming conventions and can explain the reason behind consistency rules.
primaryQuestion: What are common REST API route naming rules such as plural nouns, lowercase paths, and avoiding verbs?

expectedSignals.label: Uses nouns for resources rather than action verbs, for example /users rather than /getUsers.

requirements.description: Uses noun-based paths for resources.
Can rewrite action paths such as /getUsers into resource-oriented routes.

expectedSignals.label: Uses plural names for collections and stable item identifiers for single resources, for example /users/:id.

requirements.description: Uses plural names such as /users for collections.
Uses stable identifiers such as /users/:id for individual resources.

expectedSignals.label: Keeps paths lowercase, readable, predictable, and consistent, commonly using hyphens when a separator is needed.

requirements.description: Mentions lowercase, readable, predictable paths.
Mentions a consistent separator such as hyphens when multi-word path segments are needed.

expectedSignals.label: Uses query parameters for optional modifiers such as filters, search, sort, pagination, expansion, and field selection.

requirements.description: Uses query parameters for filters, search, sort, pagination, expansion, or field selection.
Avoids baking every optional modifier into separate route paths.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids exposing internal implementation details such as table names, service names, or storage layout.

requirements.description: Avoids route names based on database tables, service classes, or storage internals.
Uses stable public domain concepts instead of implementation-specific names.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes exceptions for domain actions that are not simple CRUD, and models them deliberately as subresources or command endpoints when needed.

requirements.description: Acknowledges actions such as suspend, approve, publish, or reset that do not fit pure CRUD neatly.
Suggests deliberate modeling such as subresources, state transitions, or command endpoints without making the whole API RPC-style.

scoringHints.description: Explains naming conventions, query usage, implementation hiding, and how to handle non-CRUD actions pragmatically.
Knows the main conventions but misses exceptions or query-parameter reasoning.
Can repeat plural nouns and lowercase but cannot apply the rules to realistic API design.
Answer produces inconsistent or action-oriented routes without rationale.

followUps.question: How would you model an action such as suspending a user without turning the whole API into RPC-style routes?
followUps.purpose: Check pragmatic handling of non-CRUD operations.

followUps.question: Give three examples of bad route names and rewrite them in a more RESTful style.

en.title: REST route naming rules
en.displayQuestion: What are common REST API route naming rules such as plural nouns, lowercase paths, and avoiding verbs?
en.displayIntent: Check route naming consistency and the reasoning behind resource-oriented URLs.
guidance: Use nouns and plural collection names.
Keep paths lowercase and consistent.
Use query parameters for optional list modifiers.
Explain how to handle non-CRUD domain actions deliberately.
commonMistakes: Using /getUsers or /deleteUser for CRUD.
Mixing singular and plural randomly.
Leaking database or service names into URLs.
Ignoring backward compatibility when renaming routes.

vi.title: Quy tắc đặt tên route REST
vi.displayQuestion: Quy tắc đặt tên route trong REST API là gì, ví dụ số nhiều, danh từ, lowercase?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra tính nhất quán khi đặt route và reasoning phía sau URL hướng resource.
guidance: Dùng danh từ và tên collection dạng số nhiều.
Giữ path lowercase và nhất quán.
Dùng query parameter cho modifier tùy chọn của danh sách.
Giải thích cách xử lý domain action không phải CRUD một cách có chủ đích.
commonMistakes: Dùng /getUsers hoặc /deleteUser cho CRUD.
Trộn singular và plural tùy tiện.
Lộ tên database hoặc service trong URL.
Đổi route mà không nghĩ tới backward compatibility.

ja.title: REST route naming rules
ja.displayQuestion: plural nouns、lowercase、verbs を避けるなど、REST API route naming の一般的なルールは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: resource-oriented URL の一貫性とその理由を確認する。
guidance: 名詞と plural collection names を使う。
path は lowercase で一貫させる。
list の optional modifier には query parameter を使う。
CRUD ではない domain action の扱いを意図的に説明する。
commonMistakes: CRUD に /getUsers や /deleteUser を使う。
singular と plural をランダムに混ぜる。
database や service の名前を URL に漏らす。
route rename 時に backward compatibility を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: Microsoft REST API Guidelines
sourceReferences.note: Used for resource naming, URL, and versioning conventions.

sourceReferences.label: Google Cloud API Design Guide: Resource names
sourceReferences.note: Used for resource-oriented naming concepts.

intent: Check whether the candidate can choose appropriate HTTP status codes for common success, client error, auth, and server error scenarios.
primaryQuestion: Which HTTP status codes should be used for created successfully, not found, server error, and unauthorized situations?

expectedSignals.label: Uses 201 Created when a resource is successfully created, ideally with Location header or the created representation when appropriate.

requirements.description: Uses 201 Created for successful resource creation.
Mentions returning a Location header or created representation when appropriate.

expectedSignals.label: Uses 404 Not Found when the target resource does not exist or should not be revealed.

requirements.description: Uses 404 when the target resource does not exist.
Mentions that 404 can be used when existence should not be revealed.

expectedSignals.label: Uses 500 Internal Server Error for unexpected server failures, while recognizing more specific 5xx codes may be appropriate for gateway or dependency failures.

requirements.description: Uses 500 for unexpected server-side failures.
Recognizes cases for more specific 5xx codes such as gateway or dependency failures.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes 401 Unauthorized as unauthenticated or invalid credentials from 403 Forbidden as authenticated but not allowed.

requirements.description: Explains 401 as missing, invalid, or unauthenticated credentials.
Explains 403 as authenticated but not permitted for the requested action.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions common adjacent codes: 200 OK, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Content, 429 Too Many Requests.

requirements.description: Mentions adjacent codes such as 200, 204, 400, 409, 422, or 429.
Connects at least some adjacent codes to concrete scenarios such as validation, conflict, or rate limiting.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that response body should provide a stable error code/message for clients without leaking internals.

requirements.description: Mentions stable machine-readable error code and human-readable message for clients.
Warns against exposing stack traces, SQL errors, or sensitive internals in error responses.

scoringHints.description: Chooses correct core status codes, distinguishes adjacent cases, and explains client-facing error contract.
Gets the four requested codes right but misses 401 versus 403 nuance or adjacent codes.
Knows a few status codes but applies them inconsistently across error types.
Status-code choices would produce misleading API behavior.

followUps.question: What is the difference between 401 and 403 in an authenticated API?
What would you return when a user tries to create an account with an email that already exists?
followUps.purpose: Probe conflict and validation status-code reasoning.

en.title: HTTP status code choices
en.displayQuestion: Which HTTP status codes should be used for created successfully, not found, server error, and unauthorized situations?
en.displayIntent: Check practical status-code selection for common REST API outcomes.
guidance: Use 201 for successful creation and 404 for missing resources.
Use 500 for unexpected server failures.
Separate 401 unauthenticated from 403 forbidden.
Mention adjacent codes such as 400, 409, 422, 429, and 204.
commonMistakes: Returning 200 with an error body.
Using 500 for validation errors.
Confusing 401 and 403.
Returning internal exception details to clients.

vi.title: Lựa chọn HTTP status code
vi.displayQuestion: HTTP status code nào dùng cho từng tình huống: tạo thành công, không tìm thấy, lỗi server, unauthorized?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng chọn status code thực tế cho các outcome phổ biến của REST API.
guidance: Dùng 201 cho tạo thành công và 404 cho resource không tồn tại.
Dùng 500 cho lỗi server không mong đợi.
Tách 401 chưa xác thực khỏi 403 đã xác thực nhưng không có quyền.
Nhắc các code gần kề như 400, 409, 422, 429 và 204.
commonMistakes: Trả 200 kèm error body.
Dùng 500 cho validation error.
Nhầm 401 và 403.
Trả chi tiết exception nội bộ cho client.

ja.title: HTTP status code の選択
ja.displayQuestion: 作成成功、not found、server error、unauthorized ではどの HTTP status code を使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: REST API の一般的な outcome に対する status-code selection を確認する。
guidance: 作成成功には 201、存在しない resource には 404 を使う。
予期しない server failure には 500 を使う。
401 unauthenticated と 403 forbidden を分ける。
400、409、422、429、204 など関連する code に触れる。
commonMistakes: error body 付きで 200 を返す。
validation error に 500 を使う。
401 と 403 を混同する。
internal exception details を client に返す。

sourceReferences.label: MDN: HTTP response status codes
sourceReferences.note: Used for status code semantics.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics
sourceReferences.note: Used for authoritative HTTP status and method semantics.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can distinguish POST, PUT, and PATCH by semantics, idempotency, resource target, and update behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between POST, PUT, and PATCH, and when would you use each?

expectedSignals.label: Explains POST as submitting data to a collection or processing endpoint where the server decides the result, commonly used for create when the server assigns the id.

requirements.description: Explains POST as submitting data to a collection or processing endpoint where the server decides the resulting resource/action.
Connects POST to creation when the server assigns the new resource id.

expectedSignals.label: Explains PUT as replacing or creating the full representation at a known resource URI and being idempotent when used correctly.

requirements.description: Explains PUT as replacing or creating a representation at a known resource URI.
States that correctly implemented PUT is idempotent and safe to retry for intended state.

expectedSignals.label: Explains PATCH as applying a partial modification to an existing resource and notes that idempotency depends on the patch document and server semantics.

requirements.description: Explains PATCH as a partial modification rather than full replacement.
Notes that PATCH may or may not be idempotent depending on the patch document and server behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that PUT usually requires the full resource representation or a clearly documented replacement contract.

requirements.description: States that PUT usually expects the full resource representation.
Explains that sending only changed fields with PUT can unintentionally remove or reset omitted fields unless documented otherwise.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions partial update formats such as JSON Merge Patch or JSON Patch when precision matters.

requirements.description: Mentions JSON Merge Patch, JSON Patch, or another documented partial update format.
Explains why a formal patch format helps define partial update semantics precisely.

expectedSignals.label: Gives examples such as POST /users, PUT /users/:id, and PATCH /users/:id.

requirements.description: Uses a concrete POST /users style example for creation.
Uses concrete PUT /users/:id and PATCH /users/:id style examples for item updates.

scoringHints.description: Explains method semantics, idempotency, create/update examples, full versus partial representation, and patch-format trade-offs.
Correctly distinguishes full and partial update but misses POST creation nuance or PATCH idempotency nuance.
Knows the rough mapping but cannot reason about retries, replacement, or partial modification contracts.
Answer would lead to ambiguous update semantics.

followUps.question: If a mobile client sends only displayName to update a user, why might PATCH be safer than PUT?
What happens if the same PUT request is retried after a network timeout?

en.title: POST versus PUT versus PATCH
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between POST, PUT, and PATCH, and when would you use each?
en.displayIntent: Check update and creation method semantics, including idempotency.
guidance: Explain POST as server-directed processing or creation under a collection.
Explain PUT as full replacement at a known URI.
Explain PATCH as partial modification.
Discuss idempotency and retry behavior.
commonMistakes: Treating the methods as style preferences.
Using PUT for partial updates without a clear contract.
Claiming PATCH is always idempotent.
Ignoring retry behavior.

vi.title: POST, PUT và PATCH
vi.displayQuestion: POST vs PUT vs PATCH khác nhau thế nào và khi nào dùng cái nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra semantics của method khi tạo/cập nhật resource, bao gồm idempotency.
guidance: Giải thích POST là xử lý do server quyết định hoặc tạo resource trong collection.
Giải thích PUT là thay thế toàn bộ resource tại URI đã biết.
Giải thích PATCH là sửa một phần resource.
Nói về idempotency và retry behavior.
commonMistakes: Xem các method chỉ là sở thích đặt style.
Dùng PUT cho partial update mà không có contract rõ.
Nói PATCH luôn idempotent.
Bỏ qua hành vi khi retry.

ja.title: POST、PUT、PATCH
ja.displayQuestion: POST、PUT、PATCH の違いは何で、どの場合に使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: resource creation/update の method semantics と idempotency を確認する。
guidance: POST は collection 配下で server が結果を決める processing または creation として説明する。
PUT は既知 URI の resource 全体置換として説明する。
PATCH は resource の部分変更として説明する。
idempotency と retry behavior を扱う。
commonMistakes: method を好みの違いとして扱う。
明確な contract なしに PUT を partial update に使う。
PATCH は常に idempotent と言い切る。
retry behavior を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN: HTTP request methods
sourceReferences.note: Used for POST, PUT, PATCH, safety, and idempotency semantics.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 5789: PATCH Method for HTTP
sourceReferences.note: Used for PATCH semantics.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands API versioning as a compatibility strategy and can compare versioning approaches with operational trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: What is API versioning, and what are common ways to version a REST API?

expectedSignals.label: Defines API versioning as a strategy for evolving contracts while protecting existing clients from breaking changes.

requirements.description: Defines versioning as a way to evolve API contracts over time.
Explains that versioning protects existing clients from breaking changes.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes breaking changes from non-breaking additive changes and mentions deprecation policy.

requirements.description: Separates breaking changes from non-breaking additive changes.
Mentions deprecation windows, migration guidance, or compatibility policy.

expectedSignals.label: Describes URI path versioning such as /v1/users, header versioning, media type versioning, query parameter versioning, and hostname/subdomain versioning.

requirements.description: Describes URI path versioning such as /v1/users.
Mentions header, media type, query parameter, hostname, or subdomain versioning.

expectedSignals.label: Compares trade-offs: path versions are visible and easy to route, headers/media types are cleaner but less discoverable, query parameters are simple but often less preferred for major versions.

requirements.description: Explains path versioning trade-offs such as visibility, routing simplicity, and URL permanence.
Explains trade-offs of headers/media types or query parameters such as discoverability, cleanliness, and client tooling.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions contract documentation, changelogs, sunset dates, monitoring client adoption, and compatibility tests.

requirements.description: Mentions OpenAPI/contract docs, changelogs, or migration notes.
Mentions sunset dates, client adoption monitoring, traffic metrics, or compatibility tests.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids versioning every small change and recommends stable contracts plus additive evolution when possible.

requirements.description: States that small additive changes do not always require a new major version.
Recommends stable contracts and backward-compatible additions when possible.

scoringHints.description: Frames versioning around client contracts, compares strategies, and includes deprecation, observability, documentation, and testing.
Knows common versioning methods and basic trade-offs but misses lifecycle management or client adoption tracking.
Thinks versioning is only adding /v2 to URLs and cannot explain when it is needed.
Answer would create avoidable client breakages.

followUps.question: When would you choose path versioning over Accept-header versioning?
How would you know it is safe to retire v1?
followUps.purpose: Probe client adoption and deprecation monitoring.

en.title: REST API versioning
en.displayQuestion: What is API versioning, and what are common ways to version a REST API?
en.displayIntent: Check compatibility thinking and versioning strategy trade-offs.
guidance: Define versioning around client-facing contract changes.
Compare path, header, media type, query parameter, and hostname versioning.
Mention deprecation, changelog, documentation, sunset, and adoption metrics.
Separate breaking changes from additive evolution.
commonMistakes: Versioning every internal implementation change.
Breaking clients without a migration window.
Mixing versioning styles randomly.
Ignoring documentation and monitoring.

vi.title: Versioning REST API
vi.displayQuestion: API versioning là gì? Có những cách nào để versioning một REST API?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra tư duy compatibility và trade-off khi chọn chiến lược versioning.
guidance: Định nghĩa versioning xoay quanh thay đổi contract mà client nhìn thấy.
So sánh path, header, media type, query parameter và hostname versioning.
Nhắc deprecation, changelog, documentation, sunset và metric adoption.
Tách breaking change khỏi additive evolution.
commonMistakes: Versioning mọi thay đổi implementation nội bộ.
Làm vỡ client mà không có migration window.
Trộn style versioning tùy tiện.
Bỏ qua documentation và monitoring.

ja.title: REST API versioning
ja.displayQuestion: API versioning とは何ですか。REST API を versioning する一般的な方法には何がありますか。
ja.displayIntent: compatibility と versioning strategy の trade-off を確認する。
guidance: client-facing contract change を中心に versioning を定義する。
path、header、media type、query parameter、hostname versioning を比較する。
deprecation、changelog、documentation、sunset、adoption metrics に触れる。
breaking change と additive evolution を分ける。
commonMistakes: internal implementation change ごとに versioning する。
migration window なしで client を壊す。
versioning style をランダムに混ぜる。
documentation と monitoring を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: Microsoft REST API Guidelines: Versioning
sourceReferences.note: Used for versioning and compatibility guidance.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 8594: Sunset HTTP Header
sourceReferences.note: Used for deprecation and sunset communication context.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can design REST pagination with clear parameters, metadata, stable ordering, and performance awareness.
primaryQuestion: How would you implement pagination in a REST API?

expectedSignals.label: Describes offset/page pagination using parameters such as page and limit or offset and limit, including total count when feasible.

requirements.description: Describes page/limit or offset/limit parameters.
Mentions total count or page metadata when feasible and not too expensive.

expectedSignals.label: Describes cursor/keyset pagination using cursor and limit for high-volume or frequently changing datasets.

requirements.description: Describes cursor/keyset pagination using cursor and limit or a last-seen key.
Explains why cursor pagination helps with high-volume or frequently changing datasets.

expectedSignals.label: Explains the need for deterministic sorting, stable tie-breakers, maximum page size, and validation of pagination parameters.

requirements.description: Mentions deterministic sorting and a stable tie-breaker such as id when sort values can repeat.
Mentions maximum page size and validation of page, offset, cursor, or limit parameters.

expectedSignals.label: Includes response metadata such as nextCursor, previousCursor, page, limit, total, hasNext, or links depending on the strategy.

requirements.description: Includes metadata such as page, limit, total, hasNext, nextCursor, previousCursor, or links.
Uses metadata appropriate to offset/page or cursor pagination rather than mixing contracts carelessly.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions performance trade-offs: large offsets can be slow and inconsistent under concurrent writes, while cursors are more complex but scale better.

requirements.description: Mentions that large offsets can become slow or inconsistent under concurrent writes.
Mentions that cursors are more complex but scale and remain more consistent for changing data.

expectedSignals.label: Covers client behavior such as preserving filters/sort in links and handling empty pages.

requirements.description: Mentions preserving filters and sort criteria in next/previous links or cursor state.
Mentions empty result pages, end-of-list behavior, or hasNext false.

scoringHints.description: Compares offset and cursor pagination, includes validation, stable sorting, metadata, and database performance trade-offs.
Provides a workable page/limit design but misses cursor or large-dataset consistency concerns.
Knows limit and offset but cannot explain metadata, sorting, or performance implications.
Pagination design would be unsafe or unusable for real clients.

followUps.question: When would you switch from offset pagination to cursor pagination?
Why does cursor pagination usually require a stable sort column and a tie-breaker?
followUps.purpose: Probe correctness under duplicate sort values.

en.title: REST pagination design
en.displayQuestion: How would you implement pagination in a REST API?
en.displayIntent: Check pagination parameters, metadata, correctness, and performance awareness.
guidance: Compare page/limit or offset/limit with cursor/keyset pagination.
Mention stable ordering, maximum limit, and validation.
Include response metadata and navigation links or cursors.
Discuss database performance and concurrent writes.
commonMistakes: Returning all records to the client.
Allowing unlimited page size.
Ignoring sort stability.
Using large offsets without considering performance.

vi.title: Thiết kế pagination cho REST
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để implement pagination trong REST API?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra parameter, metadata, correctness và awareness về performance của pagination.
guidance: So sánh page/limit hoặc offset/limit với cursor/keyset pagination.
Nhắc stable ordering, maximum limit và validation.
Bao gồm metadata response và navigation link hoặc cursor.
Nói về performance database và concurrent writes.
commonMistakes: Trả toàn bộ record cho client.
Cho phép page size không giới hạn.
Bỏ qua sort stability.
Dùng offset lớn mà không xét performance.

ja.title: REST pagination design
ja.displayQuestion: REST API で pagination をどのように実装しますか。
guidance: page/limit または offset/limit と cursor/keyset pagination を比較する。
stable ordering、maximum limit、validation に触れる。
response metadata と navigation links または cursors を含める。
database performance と concurrent writes を説明する。
commonMistakes: すべての record を client に返す。
page size を無制限にする。
sort stability を無視する。
performance を考えず large offset を使う。

sourceReferences.label: JSON:API: Pagination
sourceReferences.note: Used for pagination links and metadata concepts.

sourceReferences.label: PostgreSQL docs: LIMIT and OFFSET
sourceReferences.note: Used for offset ordering and performance considerations.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can describe a consistent REST response contract for success, errors, metadata, and traceability without over-wrapping HTTP semantics.
primaryQuestion: What should a standard response format for a REST API look like?

expectedSignals.label: Defines a consistent success shape such as data plus optional meta and links for collection responses.

requirements.description: Defines a consistent success response using data or a similarly clear payload field.
Mentions optional meta and links for collections where useful.

expectedSignals.label: Defines a consistent error shape with stable machine-readable code, human-readable message, optional field errors, requestId or traceId, and no sensitive internals.

requirements.description: Includes stable error code and human-readable message.
Mentions field errors, requestId/traceId, and avoiding sensitive internals.

expectedSignals.label: Uses HTTP status codes as the primary success/error signal instead of always returning 200 with success flags.

requirements.description: States that HTTP status code should carry the primary success/error semantics.
Avoids returning 200 for all errors with only a success flag in the body.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions response consistency across endpoints while allowing exceptions such as 204 No Content, file downloads, streaming, or problem details.

requirements.description: Explains why consistent response shapes help clients.
Mentions exceptions such as 204, file downloads, streaming, or RFC problem details.

expectedSignals.label: Includes metadata for pagination, filtering, sorting, and timestamps where useful.

requirements.description: Mentions metadata for pagination, filtering, sorting, counts, or cursors.
Keeps metadata purposeful instead of forcing it into every response.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions content type, versioning considerations, backward compatibility, and documentation in OpenAPI.

requirements.description: Mentions content type and documenting the contract in OpenAPI or similar.
Mentions keeping response changes backward compatible or versioned.

scoringHints.description: Balances consistent envelopes, HTTP semantics, error contracts, metadata, traceability, and documented exceptions.
Gives a reasonable data/error/meta format but misses traceability, exceptions, or status-code semantics.
Suggests a shape but cannot explain how clients use it or how errors stay stable.
Response contract would be inconsistent or misleading for clients.

followUps.question: How would your error response represent validation errors for multiple fields?
When would you intentionally not wrap the response in a data envelope?
followUps.purpose: Probe HTTP semantics and exception handling.

en.title: REST response format
en.displayQuestion: What should a standard response format for a REST API look like?
en.displayIntent: Check consistent success and error contracts without fighting HTTP semantics.
guidance: Describe success payloads with data, meta, and links where useful.
Describe errors with code, message, details, field errors, and requestId.
Use HTTP status codes as the primary signal.
Mention exceptions such as 204, files, and streams.
commonMistakes: Returning 200 for every outcome.
Leaking internal error details.
Changing shapes without documentation.
Wrapping responses mechanically.

vi.title: Format response REST
vi.displayQuestion: Response format chuẩn cho một REST API nên trông như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra contract success/error nhất quán mà vẫn tôn trọng HTTP semantics.
guidance: Mô tả success payload với data, meta và links khi hữu ích.
Mô tả error với code, message, details, field errors và requestId.
Dùng HTTP status code làm tín hiệu chính.
Nhắc các ngoại lệ như 204, file và stream.
commonMistakes: Trả 200 cho mọi outcome.
Lộ chi tiết lỗi nội bộ.
Thay đổi shape mà không document.
Wrap response một cách máy móc.

ja.title: REST response format
ja.displayQuestion: REST API の標準的な response format はどのような形にすべきですか。
ja.displayIntent: HTTP semantics と矛盾しない一貫した success/error contract を確認する。
guidance: 必要に応じて data、meta、links を持つ success payload を説明する。
code、message、details、field errors、requestId を持つ error を説明する。
HTTP status code を主要な signal として使う。
204、file、stream などの例外に触れる。
commonMistakes: すべての outcome に 200 を返す。
internal error details を漏らす。
document なしで shape を変える。
機械的に response を wrap する。

sourceReferences.label: RFC 9457: Problem Details for HTTP APIs
sourceReferences.note: Used for standardized error response concepts.

sourceReferences.label: JSON:API format
sourceReferences.note: Used for data, errors, meta, and links response design references.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands CORS as a browser-enforced cross-origin policy and can configure it safely for REST APIs.
primaryQuestion: What is CORS, and why does a REST API need to handle it?

expectedSignals.label: Defines CORS as Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, a browser mechanism that controls whether frontend JavaScript from one origin can read responses from another origin.

requirements.description: Defines CORS as browser-enforced cross-origin response read control.
Explains the browser JavaScript scenario of one origin calling another origin.

expectedSignals.label: Explains origin as scheme, host, and port, and distinguishes CORS from server-to-server access control.

requirements.description: Defines origin using scheme, host, and port.
States that CORS does not stop backend services, curl, Postman, or direct clients from calling the API.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions preflight OPTIONS requests for non-simple methods, custom headers, credentials, or content types.

requirements.description: Mentions browser preflight OPTIONS requests.
Names triggers such as non-simple methods, custom headers, credentials, Authorization, or content types.

expectedSignals.label: Describes key headers such as Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers, Allow-Credentials, and Vary: Origin.

requirements.description: Mentions Access-Control-Allow-Origin.
Mentions headers such as Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers, Allow-Credentials, or Vary: Origin.

expectedSignals.label: Explains safe configuration: allow only trusted origins, be careful with credentials, and avoid wildcard origins with cookies or authorization-sensitive APIs.

requirements.description: Recommends allowing only trusted origins for sensitive APIs.
Warns against wildcard origins with cookies, credentials, or authorization-sensitive APIs.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions Express implementation with the cors middleware or explicit middleware, including route-specific policies.

requirements.description: Mentions using the cors package or explicit middleware in Express.
Mentions that CORS policy can be app-wide or route-specific depending on endpoint sensitivity.

scoringHints.description: Explains browser origin policy, preflight, headers, safe credential handling, and practical Express configuration.
Understands cross-origin browser requests but misses preflight or credential nuance.
Knows CORS fixes frontend errors but cannot explain browser enforcement or safe configuration.
Answer treats CORS as a general security boundary or ignores its risks.

followUps.question: Can a backend service bypass CORS by calling the API directly? What does that imply?
Why might a browser send an OPTIONS request before the real request?

en.title: CORS for REST APIs
en.displayQuestion: What is CORS, and why does a REST API need to handle it?
en.displayIntent: Check browser cross-origin mechanics and safe API configuration.
guidance: Define origin and browser-enforced response access.
Explain preflight OPTIONS requests.
Mention CORS headers and credentials.
Describe safe allow-list configuration in Express.
commonMistakes: Treating CORS as authentication.
Thinking CORS blocks all non-browser clients.
Using wildcard origins with credentials.
Forgetting OPTIONS preflight.

vi.title: CORS cho REST API
vi.displayQuestion: CORS là gì và tại sao REST API cần xử lý nó?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cơ chế cross-origin của browser và cách cấu hình API an toàn.
guidance: Định nghĩa origin và việc browser kiểm soát quyền đọc response.
Giải thích preflight OPTIONS request.
Nhắc các CORS header và credentials.
Mô tả cấu hình allow-list an toàn trong Express.
commonMistakes: Xem CORS là authentication.
Nghĩ CORS chặn mọi client không phải browser.
Dùng wildcard origin với credentials.
Quên OPTIONS preflight.

ja.title: REST API の CORS
ja.displayQuestion: CORS とは何で、なぜ REST API はそれを扱う必要がありますか。
ja.displayIntent: browser の cross-origin mechanics と安全な API configuration を確認する。
guidance: origin と browser-enforced response access を定義する。
preflight OPTIONS request を説明する。
CORS headers と credentials に触れる。
Express での安全な allow-list configuration を説明する。
commonMistakes: CORS を authentication として扱う。
CORS がすべての non-browser clients をブロックすると考える。
credentials と wildcard origin を一緒に使う。
OPTIONS preflight を忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: MDN: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing
sourceReferences.note: Used for CORS concepts, headers, and preflight.

sourceReferences.label: Express cors middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for Express CORS configuration patterns.

intent: Check whether the candidate can validate Express request bodies safely using common validation libraries and return consistent client errors.
primaryQuestion: How do you validate a request body in Express using tools such as express-validator, Joi, or Zod?

expectedSignals.label: Treats req.body as untrusted input even after express.json parses it.

requirements.description: States that parsed req.body is still client-controlled and untrusted.
Explains that TypeScript types or interfaces do not validate runtime request data.

expectedSignals.label: Describes registering express.json before routes that need req.body and handling JSON parse errors.

requirements.description: States that express.json must be registered before routes that read req.body.
Mentions handling syntactically invalid JSON separately from schema validation failures.

expectedSignals.label: Shows a validation middleware pattern that checks required fields, types, formats, lengths, enum values, and unknown fields before controller logic.

requirements.description: Checks required fields, types, formats, length/range, enums, or unknown fields.
Places validation middleware before controller/business logic.

expectedSignals.label: Compares library styles: express-validator uses route-level validation chains, Joi/Zod use schemas and parse/validate results.

requirements.description: Mentions express-validator route-level validation chains or result extraction.
Mentions Joi/Zod schema parsing or validation result handling.

expectedSignals.label: Returns 400 or 422 with a consistent error response containing field-level errors rather than allowing invalid data into business logic.

requirements.description: Uses 400 or 422 for body validation failures.
Returns a consistent error response with field-level details instead of passing invalid data onward.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions sharing schemas/types where appropriate, sanitization when needed, and avoiding mutation surprises.

requirements.description: Mentions sharing schemas or inferred types where appropriate.
Mentions sanitization/normalization when needed and avoids surprising mutation of req.body.

scoringHints.description: Explains middleware placement, runtime schemas, field-level errors, safe status codes, and trade-offs across express-validator, Joi, and Zod.
Can implement schema validation but misses parser errors, unknown fields, or response consistency.
Understands validation is needed but keeps it ad hoc and controller-heavy.
Answer would allow untrusted or malformed input into core logic.

followUps.question: Why are TypeScript interfaces not enough to validate req.body at runtime?
followUps.purpose: Check compile-time versus runtime validation understanding.

followUps.question: What should your API return if the JSON body is syntactically invalid versus valid JSON with invalid fields?
followUps.purpose: Probe parser error and validation error distinction.

en.title: Express request body validation
en.displayQuestion: How do you validate a request body in Express using tools such as express-validator, Joi, or Zod?
en.displayIntent: Check runtime validation, middleware placement, and client error shaping.
guidance: Treat req.body as untrusted input.
Place parsers and validation middleware before controllers.
Compare express-validator chains with Joi/Zod schemas.
Return consistent field-level errors.
commonMistakes: Relying only on TypeScript types.
Doing ad hoc checks inside controllers.
Converting validation failures into 500 errors.
Ignoring unknown fields and parser errors.

vi.title: Validate request body trong Express
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để validate request body trong Express bằng express-validator, Joi hoặc Zod?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra runtime validation, vị trí middleware và cách format lỗi cho client.
guidance: Xem req.body là input không đáng tin.
Đặt parser và validation middleware trước controller.
So sánh express-validator chains với schema của Joi/Zod.
Trả lỗi field-level nhất quán.
commonMistakes: Chỉ dựa vào TypeScript type.
Check thủ công rải rác trong controller.
Biến validation failure thành 500 error.
Bỏ qua unknown fields và parser errors.

ja.title: Express request body validation
ja.displayQuestion: express-validator、Joi、Zod などを使って Express の request body をどう validate しますか。
ja.displayIntent: runtime validation、middleware placement、client error shaping を確認する。
guidance: req.body を信頼できない input として扱う。
parser と validation middleware を controller の前に置く。
express-validator chains と Joi/Zod schemas を比較する。
一貫した field-level errors を返す。
commonMistakes: TypeScript type だけに依存する。
controller 内で ad hoc check を行う。
validation failure を 500 error にする。
unknown fields と parser errors を無視する。

sourceReferences.note: Used for request body parsing and untrusted body warning.

sourceReferences.note: Used for validation chain and error result concepts.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands route/controller separation in Express and can avoid both controller bloat and unnecessary abstraction.
primaryQuestion: Why should routes and controllers be separated into different files?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that route files should define HTTP method, path, middleware composition, and controller binding.

requirements.description: States that route files define method, path, and middleware composition.
Explains that routes bind HTTP endpoints to controller functions.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that controllers should translate HTTP input/output and delegate business logic to services or use cases.

requirements.description: States that controllers translate request input and response output.
Explains that business logic should be delegated to services/use cases rather than kept in controllers.

expectedSignals.label: Connects separation to readability, testability, reuse of middleware, consistent error handling, and team ownership.

requirements.description: Explains how separation improves scanning and unit testing.
Mentions middleware reuse, consistent error handling, or clearer ownership.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that large route files become hard to scan, while large controllers with business logic become hard to test.

requirements.description: Mentions large route files becoming hard to scan or reason about.
Mentions controllers with business logic becoming hard to test and maintain.

expectedSignals.label: Acknowledges that tiny prototypes may keep things together initially, but growing systems benefit from explicit boundaries.

requirements.description: Acknowledges that tiny prototypes can keep route and controller together initially.
Explains that explicit boundaries become valuable as code, tests, or teams grow.

expectedSignals.label: Describes a practical folder layout such as routes/users.routes.ts, controllers/users.controller.ts, services/users.service.ts, and validators/users.schema.ts.

requirements.description: Gives a concrete folder/file layout separating routes, controllers, and services.
Mentions where validators or schemas can live in the layout.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates HTTP routing, controller adaptation, service logic, validation, errors, and test boundaries while staying pragmatic.
Understands separation benefits but misses service boundaries or over-abstracts small examples.
Repeats clean code terms but cannot assign responsibilities to files.
Architecture would become route-handler sprawl or abstraction without value.

followUps.question: What code belongs in a users route file, and what code belongs in a users controller?
When would you intentionally keep a route and controller together?

en.title: Routes and controllers separation
en.displayQuestion: Why should routes and controllers be separated into different files?
en.displayIntent: Check maintainable Express structure and responsibility boundaries.
guidance: Assign routes to path/method/middleware wiring.
Assign controllers to HTTP input/output adaptation.
Move business logic into services or use cases.
Explain testability, readability, and team ownership.
commonMistakes: Putting all logic in route handlers.
Creating pass-through layers with no responsibility.
Confusing controllers with services.
Treating folder separation as the goal.

vi.title: Tách routes và controllers
vi.displayQuestion: Tại sao nên tách routes và controllers thành các file riêng?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cấu trúc Express dễ maintain và ranh giới trách nhiệm.
guidance: Đặt routes phụ trách path/method/middleware wiring.
Đặt controllers phụ trách chuyển đổi HTTP input/output.
Đưa business logic vào services hoặc use cases.
Giải thích testability, readability và ownership trong team.
commonMistakes: Đưa toàn bộ logic vào route handler.
Tạo layer pass-through không có trách nhiệm thật.
Nhầm controller với service.
Xem tách folder là mục tiêu chính.

ja.title: routes と controllers の分離
ja.displayQuestion: なぜ routes と controllers を別 file に分けるべきですか。
ja.displayIntent: maintainable な Express structure と responsibility boundaries を確認する。
guidance: routes は path/method/middleware wiring を担当する。
controllers は HTTP input/output adaptation を担当する。
business logic は services または use cases に移す。
testability、readability、team ownership を説明する。
commonMistakes: すべての logic を route handlers に置く。
責任のない pass-through layers を作る。
controllers と services を混同する。
folder separation 自体を目的にする。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Routing
sourceReferences.note: Used for route and router composition.

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware responsibility and routing composition.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can explain rate limiting and design a practical implementation for REST APIs, including identity, algorithms, distributed state, and client behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is rate limiting in a REST API, and how would you implement it?

expectedSignals.label: Defines rate limiting as controlling how many requests a client, user, token, IP, tenant, or route can make within a time window to protect availability and fairness.

requirements.description: Defines limiting requests per time window for a subject such as user, token, IP, tenant, or route.
Explains protection goals such as availability, abuse control, fairness, or cost control.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions algorithms such as fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, or leaky bucket and explains basic trade-offs.

requirements.description: Mentions algorithms such as fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, or leaky bucket.
Explains basic trade-offs such as burst handling, accuracy, memory cost, or implementation simplicity.

expectedSignals.label: Describes Express implementation using middleware such as express-rate-limit for simple cases or Redis-backed storage for multi-instance deployments.

requirements.description: Mentions implementing rate limiting as Express middleware such as express-rate-limit.
Mentions Redis or another shared store for multi-instance deployments.

expectedSignals.label: Uses appropriate keys such as authenticated user id, API key, tenant, IP, route, or a combination, with care around proxies and trust proxy settings.

requirements.description: Chooses keys such as user id, API key, tenant, IP, route, or a combination.
Mentions proxy headers, IP spoofing risk, or trust proxy configuration when using IP-based limits.

expectedSignals.label: Returns 429 Too Many Requests and useful headers such as Retry-After and rate limit remaining/reset information.

requirements.description: Uses 429 Too Many Requests when the limit is exceeded.
Mentions Retry-After or remaining/reset rate limit headers for client behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions different limits per endpoint, exemptions or higher tiers for trusted clients, observability, alerting, and avoiding process-local counters in distributed systems.

requirements.description: Mentions different limits by endpoint risk, client tier, or trusted exemptions.
Mentions metrics, alerts, and avoiding process-local counters in distributed deployments.

scoringHints.description: Covers identity, algorithms, middleware, Redis/distributed state, 429 behavior, headers, endpoint-specific policies, and metrics.
Can implement basic Express rate limiting but misses distributed state or proxy/keying nuance.
Knows rate limiting exists but designs a brittle single-process counter.
Answer does not provide viable overload or abuse protection.

followUps.question: Which endpoints would you rate limit more strictly than others, and why?
What metrics would tell you the rate limit is protecting the API without hurting normal users?

en.title: REST API rate limiting
en.displayQuestion: What is rate limiting in a REST API, and how would you implement it?
en.displayIntent: Check overload protection, abuse prevention, and distributed implementation awareness.
guidance: Define limits by user, token, IP, tenant, or route.
Compare fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, and leaky bucket.
Mention Express middleware and Redis-backed stores.
Return 429 with Retry-After and useful rate limit headers.
commonMistakes: Using only local counters in a scaled deployment.
Applying one limit to all traffic.
Ignoring proxies and client identity.
Returning inconsistent errors without client guidance.

vi.title: Rate limiting cho REST API
vi.displayQuestion: Rate limiting trong REST API là gì và implement như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng bảo vệ overload, chống abuse và awareness về distributed implementation.
guidance: Định nghĩa limit theo user, token, IP, tenant hoặc route.
So sánh fixed window, sliding window, token bucket và leaky bucket.
Nhắc Express middleware và store dùng Redis.
Trả 429 kèm Retry-After và rate limit headers hữu ích.
commonMistakes: Chỉ dùng local counter trong deployment đã scale.
Áp một limit cho mọi traffic.
Bỏ qua proxy và identity của client.
Trả lỗi không nhất quán và không hướng dẫn client.

ja.title: REST API rate limiting
ja.displayQuestion: REST API における rate limiting とは何で、どのように実装しますか。
ja.displayIntent: overload protection、abuse prevention、distributed implementation awareness を確認する。
guidance: user、token、IP、tenant、route ごとの limit を定義する。
fixed window、sliding window、token bucket、leaky bucket を比較する。
Express middleware と Redis-backed store に触れる。
429 と Retry-After、rate limit headers を返す。
commonMistakes: scaled deployment で local counters だけを使う。
すべての traffic に一つの limit を適用する。
proxy と client identity を無視する。
client guidance なしに一貫しない error を返す。

sourceReferences.note: Used for rate limit response semantics.

sourceReferences.label: express-rate-limit documentation
sourceReferences.note: Used for Express middleware implementation concepts.

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Denial of Service Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for resource exhaustion and abuse mitigation framing.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands idempotent HTTP methods and why idempotency matters for retries, duplicate requests, and distributed systems.
primaryQuestion: What is an idempotent request, and which HTTP methods are idempotent?

expectedSignals.label: Defines idempotency as making the same request once or multiple times resulting in the same intended server state, even if each response may differ.

requirements.description: Defines idempotency by same intended server state after one or many identical requests.
Clarifies that response codes or bodies may differ across repeated requests.

expectedSignals.label: Identifies GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE, PUT, and DELETE as idempotent by HTTP semantics when implemented correctly.

requirements.description: Identifies GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE, PUT, and DELETE as idempotent by HTTP semantics.
Adds that implementation must preserve the expected semantics.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that POST is not generally idempotent, while PATCH may or may not be idempotent depending on the patch operation and server contract.

requirements.description: States that POST generally is not idempotent because repeated calls can create repeated effects.
Explains that PATCH idempotency depends on the patch operation and server contract.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes safe methods such as GET and HEAD from idempotent but state-changing methods such as PUT and DELETE.

requirements.description: Explains safe methods such as GET/HEAD are intended not to change resource state.
Explains that PUT and DELETE can change state while still being idempotent.

expectedSignals.label: Connects idempotency to retry behavior after timeouts, duplicate submissions, payment/order creation, and idempotency keys for non-idempotent operations.

requirements.description: Explains why idempotency matters for retries after timeouts or duplicate submissions.
Mentions idempotency keys or duplicate prevention for payments, orders, or other POST operations.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that server implementation can violate method expectations, so API contracts and tests matter.

requirements.description: Mentions that bad server implementation can violate method semantics.
Mentions documenting and testing idempotency behavior in API contracts.

scoringHints.description: Defines idempotency precisely, names method semantics, separates safe from idempotent, and discusses retries and idempotency keys.
Knows the main idempotent methods but misses response-versus-state nuance or idempotency keys.
Can name PUT as idempotent but cannot explain DELETE, PATCH, or retries.
Answer confuses safety, state change, and duplicate request behavior.

followUps.question: If DELETE /users/123 returns 204 the first time and 404 the second time, can it still be idempotent?
How would you make POST /payments safe to retry after a timeout?
followUps.purpose: Probe idempotency keys and duplicate prevention.

en.title: HTTP idempotency
en.displayQuestion: What is an idempotent request, and which HTTP methods are idempotent?
en.displayIntent: Check method semantics and retry safety reasoning.
guidance: Define idempotency by final server state, not identical response.
Name idempotent methods and separate safe methods.
Discuss POST and PATCH nuance.
Connect idempotency to retries and idempotency keys.
commonMistakes: Equating idempotent with read-only.
Thinking DELETE is not idempotent because the second response can differ.
Retrying POST blindly.
Ignoring implementation contracts.

vi.title: Idempotency trong HTTP
vi.displayQuestion: Idempotent request là gì? Method nào là idempotent?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra semantics của method và reasoning về retry safety.
guidance: Định nghĩa idempotency theo final server state, không phải response giống hệt.
Nêu các method idempotent và tách safe methods.
Nói nuance của POST và PATCH.
Liên hệ idempotency với retry và idempotency keys.
commonMistakes: Đồng nhất idempotent với read-only.
Nghĩ DELETE không idempotent vì response lần hai có thể khác.
Retry POST một cách mù quáng.
Bỏ qua contract implementation.

ja.title: HTTP idempotency
ja.displayQuestion: idempotent request とは何で、どの HTTP method が idempotent ですか。
ja.displayIntent: method semantics と retry safety reasoning を確認する。
guidance: idempotency は identical response ではなく final server state で定義する。
idempotent methods を挙げ、safe methods と分ける。
POST と PATCH の nuance を説明する。
idempotency を retries と idempotency keys に結びつける。
commonMistakes: idempotent を read-only と同一視する。
二回目の response が違うため DELETE は idempotent ではないと考える。
POST を blindly retry する。
implementation contracts を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN: HTTP request methods
sourceReferences.note: Used for safe and idempotent method semantics.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 9110: Methods
sourceReferences.note: Used for authoritative method semantics.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can integrate Swagger/OpenAPI documentation into an Express API and keep the contract useful and maintainable.
primaryQuestion: How do you integrate Swagger or OpenAPI documentation into Express?

expectedSignals.label: Explains OpenAPI as a machine-readable contract describing paths, methods, parameters, request bodies, responses, schemas, auth, and servers.

requirements.description: Defines OpenAPI as a machine-readable API contract.
Mentions paths, methods, parameters, request bodies, responses, schemas, auth, or servers.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions common Express setup using a generated or handwritten OpenAPI document served through swagger-ui-express at a docs route.

requirements.description: Mentions serving docs with swagger-ui-express or an equivalent docs route.
Mentions using a generated or handwritten OpenAPI JSON/YAML document.

expectedSignals.label: Describes ways to create the spec: YAML or JSON file, annotations with swagger-jsdoc, schema-driven generation, or contract-first design.

requirements.description: Names approaches such as YAML/JSON, swagger-jsdoc annotations, schema-driven generation, or contract-first design.
Compares at least one trade-off between writing the contract first and generating it from code.

expectedSignals.label: Includes documenting request/response schemas, validation errors, auth schemes such as bearer JWT or API keys, tags, examples, and status codes.

requirements.description: Mentions documenting request bodies, response schemas, validation errors, examples, and status codes.
Mentions auth schemes such as bearer JWT/API keys plus tags or grouping.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions keeping docs in sync with implementation through CI validation, linting, contract tests, generated clients, or deriving docs from shared schemas.

requirements.description: Mentions CI validation, OpenAPI linting, or contract tests.
Mentions generated clients or deriving docs from shared schemas to reduce drift.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions protecting or limiting docs exposure in production when necessary and versioning docs alongside API versions.

requirements.description: Mentions protecting, disabling, or limiting docs exposure in production when needed.
Mentions versioning documentation alongside API versions.

scoringHints.description: Covers OpenAPI contract concepts, Express UI integration, spec generation options, schema/status/auth docs, CI validation, and production exposure.
Can serve swagger-ui-express and write basic specs but misses drift prevention or auth/error documentation.
Knows Swagger UI exists but cannot explain OpenAPI structure or maintenance.
Documentation approach would be incomplete or quickly stale.

followUps.question: What fields would you document for POST /users beyond the path and method?
followUps.purpose: Check request body, responses, errors, and auth completeness.

followUps.question: Would you write OpenAPI contract-first or generate it from code? What trade-off are you accepting?

en.title: Swagger/OpenAPI in Express
en.displayQuestion: How do you integrate Swagger or OpenAPI documentation into Express?
en.displayIntent: Check API contract documentation, Express integration, and maintenance practices.
guidance: Define OpenAPI as a machine-readable API contract.
Describe serving Swagger UI from an Express route.
Mention YAML/JSON specs, swagger-jsdoc annotations, or schema-driven generation.
Include schemas, status codes, auth, examples, validation, and drift prevention.
commonMistakes: Documenting only the happy path.
Treating Swagger as only UI.
Letting docs drift from implementation.
Publishing sensitive internal docs accidentally.

vi.title: Swagger/OpenAPI trong Express
vi.displayQuestion: Swagger/OpenAPI documentation tích hợp vào Express như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra API contract documentation, cách tích hợp Express và practices để maintain.
guidance: Định nghĩa OpenAPI là contract API machine-readable.
Mô tả việc serve Swagger UI từ một Express route.
Nhắc YAML/JSON spec, swagger-jsdoc annotations hoặc generation từ schema.
Bao gồm schema, status code, auth, examples, validation và cách tránh docs drift.
commonMistakes: Chỉ document happy path.
Xem Swagger chỉ là UI.
Để docs lệch khỏi implementation.
Vô tình publish tài liệu endpoint nội bộ nhạy cảm.

ja.title: Express の Swagger/OpenAPI
ja.displayQuestion: Swagger/OpenAPI documentation を Express にどのように統合しますか。
ja.displayIntent: API contract documentation、Express integration、maintenance practices を確認する。
guidance: OpenAPI を machine-readable API contract として定義する。
Express route から Swagger UI を serve する方法を説明する。
YAML/JSON specs、swagger-jsdoc annotations、schema-driven generation に触れる。
schemas、status codes、auth、examples、validation、drift prevention を含める。
commonMistakes: happy path だけを document する。
Swagger を UI だけとして扱う。
docs と implementation の drift を放置する。
sensitive internal docs を誤って公開する。
